StackTrace: cktrace stopped: previous frame identical to this frame

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below allocator is ks_memcpy; decompiled body shows a custom memcpy that assumes non-overlapping regions for the vectorized path and does no bounds checking itself. Crash reason is generic ks_memcpy failure, suggesting OOB or bad pointer passed in rather than an internal check.

The caller in the stack is _dPKIHeader @00125790 (the non-trivial implementation). Decompilation shows multiple uses of ks_memcpy. The relevant one given the SEQUENCE/CONTEXT parsing is especially the case 0/1/7 copies into acStack_468 (a 1032-byte local) from param_1 + offset with length local_46c. local_46c is parsed from ASN.1 length fields via __dSEQUENCE, __dCONTEXTSPECIFIC, __get_len, etc., with no explicit check that local_46c fits into 1032 bytes. Therefore, if the parsed length exceeds 1032, ks_memcpy will overflow acStack_468, causing memory corruption.

Source pointer param_1 comes from ASN.1-encoded CMP header buffer. Its lifetime and bounds are not verified against local_46c beyond what the parsing helpers do internally (unknown), but there is clearly no size check against the destination capacity. This is classic stack-based buffer overflow (CWE-121/787) in _dPKIHeader.

The JNI bridge Java_com_koscom_cert_ca_CANative_cmpUpdate3 simply converts two Java byte arrays to native buffers (GetByteArrayElements via function pointer at *env+0x5c0) and calls CMP_Update_3(buf1, buf2, int flag). CMP_Update_3 is not decompiled here but library map shows _dPKIHeader in the same lib, and name plus flow strongly suggests CMP_Update_3 will parse ASN.1 request/response data and will ultimately call _dPKIHeader on these buffers.

On the Java side, cmpUpdate3([B[BI)I is called from business logic updatePwdCert / updatePWD; these pass arbitrary byte[] arguments from higher-level certificate update workflow. Fuzzer can supply arbitrary contents and sizes for these arrays; in real app, arrays may be attacker-controlled via network, certificate files, or server responses, all of which are untrusted and not size-restricted by Java. There is no indication of maximum length enforcement matching 1032 bytes.

Thus, an attacker who controls the ASN.1 message can set an encoded length >1032 to make _dPKIHeader memcpy more bytes than acStack_468 can hold. Because acStack_468 is on stack and the overflow is unbounded up to the array length, this is a clear, likely exploitable stack-based buffer overflow reachable from JNI and untrusted input.

Given the cryptographic / certificate context and the typical use of this library in security-sensitive apps, exploitation could lead to code execution within the app process or at minimum memory corruption / crash. No mitigations are visible at this level (no bounds check, no safe memcpy wrapper). This is therefore a high-severity memory corruption vulnerability, not a harness-only issue.
reasons: _dPKIHeader copies ASN.1-parsed data into a 1032-byte stack buffer via ks_memcpy using an unbounded length local_46c without checking it against buffer size
Length local_46c is derived from attacker-controlled ASN.1 fields in CMP messages and can exceed the destination buffer capacity, leading to stack-based buffer overflow
Reachable from Java via JNI bridge cmpUpdate3 through CMP_Update_3, which processes user/server-provided certificate data, making this a realistic exploit path

evidence.note: acStack_468 is a 1032-byte local buffer; local_46c is not checked to be <=1032
Same unbounded copy into acStack_468 using ASN.1-derived length
Raw memcpy implementation with no internal bounds check; relies entirely on caller to provide safe length
JNI bridge takes Java byte[] and passes raw buffers into CMP_Update_3 without any size constraints or validation

Java call graph shows cmpUpdate3 invoked as part of certificate password update; supplied byte arrays ultimately originate from external certificate data and user input without explicit maximum size checks matching native limits

recommendations: Add explicit bounds checks in _dPKIHeader before each ks_memcpy into acStack_468, ensuring local_46c does not exceed sizeof(acStack_468) and abort/return error on violation
Prefer using a safer memcpy wrapper that enforces destination buffer size or refactor ASN.1 parsing to operate on caller-supplied buffers with explicit length parameters instead of fixed-size locals
Review all uses of ks_memcpy and related parsing functions in libCertJavaClient.so for similar unbounded copies from ASN.1/attacker-controlled data
assumptions: CMP_Update_3 eventually calls _dPKIHeader with a pointer into attacker-influenced CMP/ASN.1 data buffers
Java layer does not enforce strict maximum sizes on the byte[] arguments passed into cmpUpdate3 beyond typical platform limits
limitations: Exact internal implementation of CMP_Update_3 is not decompiled here; linkage to _dPKIHeader inferred from naming/context
Java sources for KSCmp/KSNative were not fully inspected for size validation logic, but call graph suggests direct forwarding of arrays
